title: KV Cache, Flash Attention & Inference Optimization

questions.question: Naive autoregressive decoding recomputes full attention over the growing prefix at every step, costing O(N²) total operations for an N-token response. What property of autoregressive decoding makes this reducible to O(N) total work?
options: Each prefix token's K and V vectors are fully determined once computed and never change — they can be stored after the first forward pass and reused for every subsequent generation step, reducing attention per step from O(prefix_len²) to O(prefix_len) via the KV cache
Q vectors are shared between the encoder and decoder, so the prefix only needs encoding once per request, not per token
The softmax distribution over the prefix is constant once the model is frozen, so it can be cached and replayed
Batching multiple sequences together amortises the attention cost across the batch, turning O(N²) per sequence into O(N) per token
questions.explanation: In causal language model decoding, every prefix token's key K_t and value V_t are pure functions of that token and the weights — they do not depend on future tokens. The KV cache stores them on the first forward pass. Each new token generates one new Q and needs to attend to prefix K/V vectors, but those are already computed. Step cost drops from O(current_prefix_len²) to O(current_prefix_len) × constant per new token. Total cost for N tokens: O(N) instead of O(N²).

questions.question: Standard attention writes three N×N matrices to GPU HBM: scores, softmax output, and the final weighted sum. Flash Attention produces bit-identical output without materialising any of these. What is its core technique?
options: Flash Attention quantises scores to INT4 before writing to HBM, reducing bandwidth by 4× but introducing approximation error
Flash Attention reformulates softmax as a linear operation that requires no normalisation term, eliminating the N×N intermediate

Flash Attention tiles Q, K, V into SRAM-sized blocks and computes attention entirely within each tile using a running-max/sum algorithm, so the N×N score matrix is never written to HBM — total memory footprint is O(N) instead of O(N²)
Flash Attention reorders the matmul dimensions so each output row can be computed from a single K row, eliminating the broadcast over all keys
questions.explanation: SRAM bandwidth (~30 TB/s on H100) is 10× faster than HBM bandwidth (~3 TB/s). Flash Attention keeps all intermediate matrices in SRAM by processing attention in tiles: load Q_tile, K_tile, V_tile; compute S_tile in SRAM; update running (max, sum, accumulator); write only the final O_tile to HBM. The backward pass recomputes the softmax from stored log-sum-exp scalars rather than storing the N×N matrix. Output is bit-identical to standard attention (subject to fp16 non-associativity).

questions.question: For Llama 3 70B (80 layers, 8 KV heads via GQA, d_head=128, fp16), the doc computes the KV cache as approximately 10.4 GB at 32K context. Standard MHA with 64 heads would be roughly 83 GB. What feature is responsible for the 8× reduction?
options: Llama 3 uses INT8 quantisation for its KV cache, halving the bytes per head from FP16
Llama 3 uses sparse attention that only stores the top-16 activated KV pairs per layer
Llama 3 uses MLA (Multi-head Latent Attention) which compresses the KV cache into a low-rank projection

GQA (Grouped-Query Attention) uses 8 KV heads shared across all 64 Q heads — only 8 K and 8 V vectors are stored per token per layer instead of 64, directly scaling down cache from 64 heads to 8 heads
questions.explanation: MHA stores one K and one V per attention head per token. At 64 heads, d_head=128, fp16: 2 × 64 × 128 × 2 = 32 KB/token/layer. GQA with 8 KV heads: 2 × 8 × 128 × 2 = 4 KB/token/layer — an 8× reduction. The doc's arithmetic: 4 KB × 80 layers × 32768 tokens = 10.49 GB. This is why GQA became standard in every major 2023–2026 model: KV cache size is often the binding memory constraint at long context.

questions.question: Classic batched inference waits for the slowest sequence in a batch to finish before admitting new requests. What does continuous batching change, and what is the documented throughput improvement for typical chat workloads?
options: Continuous batching pre-loads the next batch in HBM while the current batch computes, removing batch-loading latency — approximately 2× improvement

Continuous batching slots new requests into vacant positions in the batch as soon as any sequence finishes, without waiting for the full batch to drain — 5–10× throughput improvement for typical chat workloads
Continuous batching trains and serves simultaneously, using gradient checkpointing to share activations between training and inference passes — 3× improvement
Continuous batching uses KV cache compression to batch more sequences simultaneously — 4× improvement by fitting twice as many contexts in GPU memory
questions.explanation: Classic batching: a 10-sequence batch completes when the last sequence generates its final token. If 9 of 10 sequences finish at token 50 and one runs to token 200, the GPU is at 1/10th utilisation for 150 steps. Continuous batching (Orca, vLLM): as soon as any sequence finishes, a queued request takes its slot. GPU utilisation approaches 100% of memory capacity, not 100% of batch capacity. The 5–10× figure assumes typical chat distribution with short median length but heavy tail.

questions.question: The Build code's tiled_softmax_dot maintains a running (m, s) state: m is the running max, s is the running normalised sum. Why does the one-pass running-max algorithm produce the same result as a two-pass approach (pass 1: find global max; pass 2: compute softmax)?
options: It processes K in chunks so the total bandwidth is O(N/tile) instead of O(N), which coincidentally produces the same output due to the distributive property of softmax
The running-max algorithm is an approximation that matches the two-pass result only when tile size equals N — in practice the small approximation error is below fp16 epsilon and therefore invisible

When each new tile arrives with max new_m, the previous partial sum s and accumulator out are rescaled by exp(m − new_m), exactly reweighting the already-seen contributions as if new_m had been the global max all along — mathematically identical to knowing the global max upfront
The running max converges to the true softmax only on sequences longer than 64 tokens — for shorter sequences a direct softmax is used as fallback
questions.explanation: Softmax denominator for a tile with local max m_tile: sum(exp(x_i − m_tile)). When the global max turns out to be m_global > m_tile, rescale by exp(m_tile − m_global). The running-max trick does this incrementally: when new_m > m, multiply the accumulated s and out by exp(m − new_m) before adding the new tile's contribution. The final result is mathematically identical to computing exp(x_i − global_max) for all i at once. This is why Flash Attention's output is called 'exact' — not an approximation.

questions.question: PagedAttention (vLLM) stores KV cache in fixed 16-token blocks with a page table. The doc claims 4× throughput improvement over naive contiguous KV allocation. What failure mode of contiguous allocation does PagedAttention fix, beyond just fragmentation?
options: Contiguous allocation forces all sequences to have the same maximum context length, wasting memory for shorter sequences and limiting batch size
Contiguous allocation cannot use fp8 for KV cache values while PagedAttention can, since the block boundaries align with fp8 quantisation boundaries

Contiguous allocation reserves peak-possible context for each sequence upfront; PagedAttention allocates blocks on demand (freeing them on completion) and enables KV block sharing across parallel beam-search or prompt-cached requests — reducing waste and increasing effective batch size
Contiguous allocation copies the full KV cache to CPU between prefill and decode stages, while PagedAttention keeps it on GPU throughout
questions.explanation: Naive contiguous allocation reserves max_context × d_kv × n_layers bytes per sequence when it starts. A 2K-token actual response in a 32K-context-window model wastes 93% of its allocation. PagedAttention allocates 16-token blocks as needed; when the sequence finishes, all blocks return to the pool immediately. Additionally, a block containing a shared prefix (same system prompt, RAG document) can be mapped into multiple sequences' page tables without duplication — prefix caching across requests is a direct consequence.
